subject: Mathematics
q: The line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>y</mi><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>60</mn></math> is tangent to which of the following curves ?
question: The line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>y</mi><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>60</mn></math> is tangent to which of the following curves ?
solution:
Given that 12x cosθ+5y sinθ=60

Divide the above equation with 60 on both sides

x cosθ5+y sinθ12=1…i

 Let ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1

⇒ Equation of tangent at point (acosθ,bsinθ)
xa cosθa2+yb sinθb2=1

xcosθa+ysinθb=1…ii

Using equation i and ii⇒a=5
b=12
Equation of ellipse is x225+y2144=1

subject: Mathematics
q: If the distance between the foci of an ellipse is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> and the distance between its directrix is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the length of its latus rectum is
question: If the distance between the foci of an ellipse is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> and the distance between its directrix is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the length of its latus rectum is
solution:
Given, 2ae=6 and 2ae=12, directrices are x=±ae

So, ae=6

Now, using the formula for eccentricity of an ellipse

⇒b2=a2-a2e2=18-9=9

⇒L.R.=2b2a=2×932=32

subject: Mathematics
q: The centre of a circle C is at the centre of the ellipse $E: \frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1, a \gt b$. Let $C$ pass through the foci $F_1$ and $F_2$ of $E$ such that the circle $C$ and the ellipse $E$ intersect at four points. Let P be one of these four points. If the area of the triangle $\mathrm{PF}_1 \mathrm{~F}_2$ is 30 and the length of the major axis of E is 17 , then the distance between the foci of E is :
question: The centre of a circle C is at the centre of the ellipse $E: \frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1, a \gt b$. Let $C$ pass through the foci $F_1$ and $F_2$ of $E$ such that the circle $C$ and the ellipse $E$ intersect at four points. Let P be one of these four points. If the area of the triangle $\mathrm{PF}_1 \mathrm{~F}_2$ is 30 and the length of the major axis of E is 17 , then the distance between the foci of E is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{PF}_1 \cdot \mathrm{PF}_2=30 \\ & \mathrm{PF}_1+\mathrm{PF}_2=17 \\ & \mathrm{PF}_1=12 \mathrm{PF}_2=5 \\ & \mathrm{~F}_1 \mathrm{~F}_2=13 \\ & \text { option }(2)\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider an ellipse, whose center is at the origin and its major axis is along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. If its eccentricity is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and the distance between its foci is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math>, then the area (in sq. units) of the quadrilateral inscribed in the ellipse, with the vertices as the vertices of the ellipse, is:
question: Consider an ellipse, whose center is at the origin and its major axis is along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. If its eccentricity is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and the distance between its foci is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math>, then the area (in sq. units) of the quadrilateral inscribed in the ellipse, with the vertices as the vertices of the ellipse, is:
solution:
Given eccentricity of the ellipse e = 3 5 .

Distance between foci  is  2ae=6
⇒ a = 5.

Also,  b 2 = a 2 (1− e 2 )

⇒ b2=251-925

⇒ b=4

Area =412ab=2ab=40

subject: Mathematics
q: If the tangents on the ellipse <math><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math> at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1,</mn><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>)</mo></math> are perpendicular to each other, then <math><msup><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> is equal to
question: If the tangents on the ellipse <math><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math> at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1,</mn><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>)</mo></math> are perpendicular to each other, then <math><msup><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> is equal to
solution:
4x2+y2=8
⇒8x+2ydydx=0
dydx=-8x2y=-4xy
∴ Slope at 1, 2, m1=-2
      Slope at a,b, m2=-4ab
∴ -2×-4ab=-1
       b=-8a
Again,
     4a2+b2=8
⇒4a2+64a2=8
⇒68a2=8
⇒a2=868

⇒a2=217

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> be a directrix to an ellipse whose centre is at the origin and its eccentricity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> is a point on this ellipse, then the equation of the normal to it at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> be a directrix to an ellipse whose centre is at the origin and its eccentricity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> is a point on this ellipse, then the equation of the normal to it at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is
solution:
ae=4⇒a=4e⇒a=2

b2=a21-e2=3

(1,β) lies on x24+y23=1⇒14+β23=1⇒    β2=94⇒β=32(∵β>0)

Normal at (1,β)⇒ a2x1-b2yβ=a2-b2  ⇒4x-3yβ=1

so equation of normal is 4x-2y=1

subject: Mathematics
q: The locus of the mid-point of the line segment joining the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the points on the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is an ellipse with eccentricity
question: The locus of the mid-point of the line segment joining the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the points on the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is an ellipse with eccentricity
solution:
Given,

x24+y22=1

Let coordinate of D 2cosθ+42,2sinθ+32≡h,k

So, 2h-42=cosθ      ...1

And 2k-32=sinθ      ...2

Equation 12+equation 22, then we get

2h-422+2k-322=1⇒x-221+y-32212=1

∴  Required eccentricity is e=1-12=12

subject: Mathematics
q: If the normal to the ellipse <math><msup><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn></math> at a point <math><mi>P</mi></math> on it is parallel to the line, <math><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and the tangent to the ellipse at <math><mi>P</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>Q</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>4,4</mn><mo>)</mo></math> then <math><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is equal to:
question: If the normal to the ellipse <math><msup><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn></math> at a point <math><mi>P</mi></math> on it is parallel to the line, <math><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and the tangent to the ellipse at <math><mi>P</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>Q</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>4,4</mn><mo>)</mo></math> then <math><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Given the equation of an ellipse is x24+y23=1,where a=2, b=3.
As we know equation of normal to the ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1 can be taken as
axsec⁡θ-bycosec⁡θ=a2-b2
⇒2xsec⁡θ-3ycosec⁡θ=1 for the given ellipse.
Now, this normal is parallel to 2x+y=5.
⇒-2sec⁡θ3cosec⁡θ=-2⇒tan⁡θ=-3⇒θ=2π3,-π3
Hence point Pacos⁡θ, bsin⁡θ=-1,32 or 1, -32
Tangent at -1,32 is x.-14+y.323=1
⇒-x+2y=4 which also passes through Q4, 4
⇒PQ=4±12+4∓322=552.

subject: Mathematics
q: The length of the minor axis (along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis) of an ellipse in the standard form is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>. If this ellipse touches the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math> then its eccentricity is:
question: The length of the minor axis (along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis) of an ellipse in the standard form is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>. If this ellipse touches the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math> then its eccentricity is:
solution:
Let equation of ellipse be x2a2+y2b2=1.

Here, 2b=43⇒b=23

Equation of tangents ≡y=mx±a2m2+b2

Comparing with ≡y=-x6+43

m=-16 and a2m2+b2=169

⇒a236+43=169

⇒a236=169-43=49

⇒a2=16

Now, e=1-b2a2

⇒e=1-43×16=1112

⇒e=12113

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> be a focal chord of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> such that it subtends an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Let the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> be also a focal chord of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>></mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> is the eccentricity of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mi>e</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> be a focal chord of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> such that it subtends an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Let the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> be also a focal chord of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>></mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> is the eccentricity of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mi>e</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math> is equal to
solution:
Since PQ is focal chord of the parabola y2=4ax

so let Pt2,2t, Q1t2,-2t and R3,0

Given mPR·mPQ=-1

2tt2-3×-2t1t2-3=-1

t2-12=0

⇒t=1

i.e. PQ must be the latus rectum

We get P1,2 & Q1,-2

For ellipse

2b2a=4 & ae=1

Also b2=a21-e2

∴ a=1+2

and e2=3-22

Hence 1e2=13-22=3+22

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the line <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi></math> and the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> intersect at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> in the first quadrant. If the normal to this ellipse at <math><mi>P</mi></math> meets the co-ordinate axes at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to
question: Let the line <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi></math> and the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> intersect at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> in the first quadrant. If the normal to this ellipse at <math><mi>P</mi></math> meets the co-ordinate axes at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Let P be x1,y1

Equation of normal at P is x2x1-yy1=-12

It passes through

-132, 0⇒-162x1=-12⇒x1=132

So, y1=223 (as P lies in Ist quadrant)

So, β=y12=23

subject: Mathematics
q: A line passing through the point $\mathrm{P}(\sqrt{5}, \sqrt{5})$ intersects the ellipse $\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{36}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{25}=1$ at $A$ and $B$ such that $(P A) .(P B)$ is maximum. Then $5\left(P A^2+P B^2\right)$ is equal to :
question: A line passing through the point $\mathrm{P}(\sqrt{5}, \sqrt{5})$ intersects the ellipse $\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{36}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{25}=1$ at $A$ and $B$ such that $(P A) .(P B)$ is maximum. Then $5\left(P A^2+P B^2\right)$ is equal to :
solution:
Given ellipse is $\frac{x^2}{36}+\frac{y^2}{25}=1$

Any point on line $A B$ can be assumed as
$\mathrm{Q}(\sqrt{5}+\mathrm{r} \cos \theta, \sqrt{5}+\mathrm{r} \sin \theta)$
Putting this in equation of ellipse, we get
$25(\sqrt{5}+r \cos \theta)^2+36(\sqrt{5}+r \sin \theta)^2=900$
Simplifying, we get
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{r}^2\left(25 \cos ^2 \theta+36 \sin ^2 \theta\right)+2 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{r}(25 \cos \theta+36 \sin \theta)-595=0 \\ & \quad|\mathrm{r}|=\mathrm{PA}, \mathrm{~PB}
\end{aligned}$
$\text { Thus, } \begin{aligned}
\text { PA } \cdot \mathrm{PB} & =\frac{595}{25 \cos ^2 \theta+36 \sin ^2 \theta}=\frac{595}{25+11 \sin ^2 \theta} \\ & =\text { maximum, if } \sin ^2 \theta=0
\end{aligned}$
This means line $A B$ must be parallel to $x$-axis
$\Rightarrow y_A=y_B=\sqrt{5}$
Putting $\mathrm{y}=\sqrt{5}$ in equation of ellipse, we get
$\frac{x^2}{36}+\frac{1}{5}=1 \Rightarrow x^2=36 \cdot \frac{4}{5}$
Hence,
$\mathrm{PA}^2+\mathrm{PB}^2=\left(\sqrt{5}-\frac{12}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2+\left(\sqrt{5}+\frac{12}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2$
$\begin{aligned} & =2\left(5+\frac{144}{5}\right)=\frac{338}{5} \\ & 5\left(\mathrm{PA}^2+\mathrm{PB}^2\right)=338\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the minimum area of the triangle formed by a tangent to the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and the co-ordinate axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to ___________.
question: If the minimum area of the triangle formed by a tangent to the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and the co-ordinate axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to ___________.
solution:
Equation of tangent at parametric point 

 b cosθ,2a sin θ  to the ellipse is xbcos θ+y2asin θ=1

Co-ordinates of Pbcos θ,0 and Q0,2asin θ respectively.

Therefore, Area of triangle formed by tangent and co-ordinate axes=12×bcos θ×2asin θ=absin θ.cos θ=2absin 2θ

as sin θ≤1 therefore we can say Minimum area of triangle =2ab

⇒k=2

subject: Mathematics
q: The eccentricity of an ellipse $E$ with centre at the origin $O$ is $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and its directrices are $x = \pm \dfrac{4\sqrt{6}}{3}$. Let $H: \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ be a hyperbola whose eccentricity is equal to the length of semi-major axis of $E$, and whose length of latus rectum is equal to the length of minor axis of $E$. Then the distance between the foci of $H$ is :
question: The eccentricity of an ellipse $E$ with centre at the origin $O$ is $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and its directrices are $x = \pm \dfrac{4\sqrt{6}}{3}$. Let $H: \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ be a hyperbola whose eccentricity is equal to the length of semi-major axis of $E$, and whose length of latus rectum is equal to the length of minor axis of $E$. Then the distance between the foci of $H$ is :
solution:
For the ellipse $E$, the eccentricity is $e_E = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and the directrices are $x = \pm \dfrac{a_E}{e_E} = \pm \dfrac{4\sqrt{6}}{3}$.

The semi-major axis $a_E$ is given by:

$a_E = e_E \times \dfrac{4\sqrt{6}}{3} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \dfrac{4\sqrt{6}}{3} = \dfrac{12\sqrt{2}}{6} = 2\sqrt{2}$

The semi-minor axis $b_E$ is given by:

$b_E^2 = a_E^2(1 - e_E^2) = (2\sqrt{2})^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{3}{4}\right) = 8 \times \dfrac{1}{4} = 2 \Rightarrow b_E = \sqrt{2}$

For the hyperbola $H: \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$, its eccentricity $e_H$ is equal to the semi-major axis of $E$:

$e_H = a_E = 2\sqrt{2}$

The length of the latus rectum of $H$ is equal to the length of the minor axis of $E$ ($2b_E$):

$\dfrac{2b^2}{a} = 2b_E = 2\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow b^2 = \sqrt{2}a$

Using the standard relation for a hyperbola $b^2 = a^2(e_H^2 - 1)$, we substitute $b^2$ and $e_H$:

$\sqrt{2}a = a^2((2\sqrt{2})^2 - 1)$

$\sqrt{2}a = a^2(8 - 1) = 7a^2$

Since $a > 0$, dividing by $a$ gives:

$a = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{7}$

The distance between the foci of the hyperbola $H$ is $2ae_H$:

$2ae_H = 2 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{7} \times 2\sqrt{2} = \dfrac{8}{7}$

Answer: $\dfrac{8}{7}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let an ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>></mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, passes through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and has eccentricity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>. If a circle, centered at focus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>, intersects <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> at two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msup><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to :
question: Let an ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>></mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, passes through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and has eccentricity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>. If a circle, centered at focus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>, intersects <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> at two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msup><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to :
solution:
The ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1 passes through the point 32,1

So, 32a2+1b2=1   ...1 and 1-b2a2=13   ...2

On solving equations 1 and 2, we get

⇒a2=3 & b2=2

⇒x23+y22=1   ...3

We know that, focii of the ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1 is ±ae, 0

Here, a=3 & e=13

∴ Its focus is 1,0 ∵α>0

Now, equation of circle is

(x-1)2+y2=43   ...4

Solving 3 and 4 we get

y=±23,x=1

⇒PQ2=1-12+23+232

=432=163

subject: Mathematics
q: If the radius of the largest circle with centre <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> inscribed in the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>36</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
question: If the radius of the largest circle with centre <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> inscribed in the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>36</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
solution:
Given equation of ellipse is x236+y29=1

It is of the form x2a2+y2b2=1

With a=6, b=3.

The centre of the circle is C2,0.

Let Pacosθ,bsinθ be the common point for ellipse and circle.

P≡6cosθ,3sinθ

Equation of normal to the ellipse will be axcosθ-bysinθ=a2-b2.

⇒6xsecθ-3ycosecθ=36-9

But the normal through P passes through the centre of the circle C2,0.

⇒62secθ-30cosecθ=27

⇒secθ=2712

⇒cosθ=49 and sinθ=659

P≡6×49,3×659

P≡83,653

Now CP=r=2-832+0-6532

⇒r2=699

⇒12r2=12×699=92

Therefore, the required answer is 92

q: Let $f(x)=x^2+9, g(x)=\frac{x}{x-9}$ and $\mathrm{a}=f \circ g(10), \mathrm{b}=g \circ f(3)$. If $\mathrm{e}$ and $l$ denote the eccentricity and the length of the latus rectum of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a}+\frac{y^2}{b}=1$, then $8 \mathrm{e}^2+l^2$ is equal to.
question: Let $f(x)=x^2+9, g(x)=\frac{x}{x-9}$ and $\mathrm{a}=f \circ g(10), \mathrm{b}=g \circ f(3)$. If $\mathrm{e}$ and $l$ denote the eccentricity and the length of the latus rectum of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a}+\frac{y^2}{b}=1$, then $8 \mathrm{e}^2+l^2$ is equal to.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the tangent and normal at the point 33,1 on the ellipse x236+y24=1 meet the y-axis at the points A and B respectively. Let the circle C be drawn taking AB as a diameter and the line x=25 intersect C at the points P and Q. If the tangents at the points P and Q on the circle intersect at the point α,β, then α2-β2 is equal to
question: Let the tangent and normal at the point 33,1 on the ellipse x236+y24=1 meet the y-axis at the points A and B respectively. Let the circle C be drawn taking AB as a diameter and the line x=25 intersect C at the points P and Q. If the tangents at the points P and Q on the circle intersect at the point α,β, then α2-β2 is equal to
solution:
Given that x236+y24=1

Observe that 33,1 lies on the given ellipse.

Equation of tangent to the ellipse at the given point is T:33x36+y4=1

⇒3x12+y4=1

Equation of normal passing through same point will be N:x-333336=y-114

⇒12x-3633=4y-4

⇒3x-93=3y-3

⇒3x-3y=83

Now tangent and normal intersects the y-axis at A and B respectively.

A0,4     B0,-8

Equation of circle with AB as diameter will be

x-x1x-x2+y-y1y-y2=0

⇒x2+(y-4)(y+8)=0

Line x=25 intersects the circle.

⇒20+y2+4y-32=0

⇒y2+4y-12=0

⇒(y+6)(y-2)=0

Hence P(25,-6)  Q(25,2)

Now let us find the equation of tangents to the circle at P,Q.

⇒T:xx1+yy1+2y+2y1-32=0

⇒T1:25x-6y+2y-12-32=0

⇒25x-4y=44

⇒T1:5x-2y=22........i

⇒T2:25x+2y+2y+4-32=0

⇒25x+4y=28

⇒T2:5x+2y=14.........ii

From i and ii

α=185  β=-2

⇒α2-β2=3045

Hence this is the required option.

subject: Mathematics
q: If a tangent to the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> meets the tangents at the extremities of its major axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the circle with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> as diameter passes through the point.
question: If a tangent to the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> meets the tangents at the extremities of its major axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the circle with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> as diameter passes through the point.
solution:
We have, x24+y21=1

Equation of tangent is cosθx+2sinθy=2

B-2,1+cosθsinθ,C2,1-cosθsinθ

≡B-2,cotθ2,C2,tanθ2

Equation of required circle will be

x+2x-2+y-cotθ2y-tanθ2=0

⇒x2-4+y2-tanθ2+cotθ2y+1=0

Hence, 3,0 satisfies the above equation.

subject: Mathematics
q: The ellipse $x^2+4 y^2=4$ is inscribed in a rectangle aligned with the coordinate axes, which in turn in inscribed in another ellipse that passes through the point $(4,0)$. Then the equation of the ellipse is
question: The ellipse $x^2+4 y^2=4$ is inscribed in a rectangle aligned with the coordinate axes, which in turn in inscribed in another ellipse that passes through the point $(4,0)$. Then the equation of the ellipse is
solution: $$ x^2+4 y^2=4 \Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{y^2}{1}=1 \Rightarrow a=2, b=1 \Rightarrow P=(2,1) $$ Required Ellipse is $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{4^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ $(2,1)$ lies on it $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{4}{16}+\frac{1}{b^2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{b^2}=1-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow b^2=\frac{4}{3} \\ & \therefore \frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{3 y^2}{4}=1 \Rightarrow x^2+12 y^2=16 \end{aligned} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> be a common tangent line to the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>36</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>31</mn></math>. Then the square of the slope of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is ______.<gwmw style="display:none;"></gwmw>
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> be a common tangent line to the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>36</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>31</mn></math>. Then the square of the slope of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is ______.<gwmw style="display:none;"></gwmw>
solution:
Given curves are x29+y24=1

x2+y2=314

let slope of common tangent be m

so tangents are y=mx±9m2+4

y=mx±3121+m2

hence 9m2+4=3141+m2

⇒36m2+16=31+31m2⇒m2=3

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\alpha x+\beta y=109$ is the equation of the chord of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$, whose mid point is $\left(\frac{5}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to :
question: If $\alpha x+\beta y=109$ is the equation of the chord of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$, whose mid point is $\left(\frac{5}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to :
solution: Equation of chord T $=\mathrm{S}_1$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{5}{2}\left(\frac{x}{9}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{4}\right)=\frac{25}{36}+\frac{1}{16} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{5 \mathrm{x}}{18}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{8}=\frac{100+9}{144}=\frac{109}{144} \\ & \Rightarrow 40 \mathrm{x}+18 \mathrm{y}=109 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=40, \beta=18 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=58 \end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be a parabola with vertex  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and directrix <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn></math>. Let an ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mi>b</mi></math> of eccentricity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math> pass through the focus of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. Then the square of the length of the latus rectum of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>, is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be a parabola with vertex  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and directrix <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn></math>. Let an ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mi>b</mi></math> of eccentricity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math> pass through the focus of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. Then the square of the length of the latus rectum of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>, is
solution:
Let Z be the foot of perpendicular from vertex to directrix of parabola,

Now, finding Z we get,

⇒x-22=y-31=-4+3-64+1

⇒x-22=y-31=-15

⇒x-22=-15, y-31=-15

⇒x=125, y=165

⇒Z≡125,165

Eccentricity of ellipse is given as 12.

Now, finding b2 using eccentricity formula we get,

⇒b2=a21-e2=a22

So, equation of ellipse will be,

⇒14425a2+25625×a22=1 {as x,y≡125,165 }

⇒14425a2+51225a2=1

⇒65625a2=1

⇒a2=65625

⇒b2=32825

Length of latus rectum is given by,
L=2b2a.

⇒L=2×328256565

⇒L=6565

⇒L2=65625

subject: Mathematics
q: In an ellipse, with centre at the origin, if the difference of the lengths of major axis and minor axis is 10 and one of the foci is at <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0,5</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then the length of its latus rectum is:
question: In an ellipse, with centre at the origin, if the difference of the lengths of major axis and minor axis is 10 and one of the foci is at <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0,5</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then the length of its latus rectum is:
solution:
Given, difference of the lengths of major and minor axis is 10.
⇒2b-2a=10 … (1)
One of the focii is 0, 53
⇒be=53 … (2)
Also a2=b21-e2 .......(3)
⇒a2=b2-75
⇒b-aa+b=75
⇒a+b=15 … (4)
From (1) and (4)
a=5 and b=10
Hence length of the latus rectum =2a2b=2⋅2510=5
